skill.name: backend-dev-guidelines
skill.description: Backend development guidelines for Node.js, Express, and TypeScript services. The skill covers layered architecture, controllers, services, repositories, middleware, validation, configuration, monitoring, and testing patterns.
skill.summary: Apply consistent backend architecture patterns for Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Prisma, Zod, and Sentry services.
skill.author: diet103

security_audit.summary: AI review found no prompt injection, executable scripts, malicious network calls, or credential exfiltration behavior. Most static findings are false positives caused by Markdown code examples, inline backticks, relative TypeScript imports, local URLs, and configuration examples. One publishable medium-risk concern remains: the Sentry monitoring example enables rich request and local variable capture, so users should review privacy settings before production use.

medium_findings.title: Monitoring Example Captures Rich Request Context
medium_findings.description: The Sentry example enables local variable capture and request data, headers, IP address, query string, URL, and user fields. The same example scrubs authorization, cookies, and email addresses, so this appears to be legitimate monitoring guidance rather than exfiltration, but production users should reduce captured data for sensitive services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is clearly presented as a Sentry configuration template and includes some scrubbing. The privacy risk is still real because request bodies, headers, query strings, IP addresses, and local variables can contain sensitive data.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command detections primarily match Markdown code fences, inline code, prose, and example shell commands. No executable skill script or instruction to run untrusted commands was found during review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are Markdown examples and documentation text, not runnable skill code. The skill directory contains only SKILL.md and resource Markdown files.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable and Secret Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The skill discusses process.env, .env files, database passwords, JWT secrets, and key files to teach centralized configuration and secret hygiene. The examples do not read the evaluator environment or transmit credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding sections explicitly discourage scattered process.env usage and advise not committing secrets. The detected patterns are illustrative configuration snippets.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Path Traversal Findings Are Relative Import Examples
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections mostly match normal TypeScript relative imports and example config file reads inside Markdown. No dynamic user-controlled path construction or runtime filesystem access by the skill was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are static documentation snippets using common imports such as ../controllers and fixed config paths. There is no evidence of user input flowing into file paths.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Local Test and Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are localhost examples and placeholder Sentry or Keycloak configuration values. No hidden outbound endpoint or unauthorized network request by the skill was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in configuration and test command examples for local development. They are not part of executable skill logic.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: A targeted review for override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, and instructions to skip analysis found no evidence of prompt injection in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted searches found only ordinary documentation language, including a normal code comment that says never fire and forget. No suspicious authority or override text was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Sentry Data Capture Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The monitoring guide shows a Sentry configuration that captures local variables and detailed request data. This is useful for debugging but can expose sensitive values unless teams reduce the capture scope and scrub data carefully.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in a production-style template. It is not malicious, but it is a security-relevant implementation choice.

content.value_statement: Backend services often drift into mixed routing, business logic, and configuration patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for layered Node.js and TypeScript services.
actual_capabilities: Explains layered Express architecture with routes, controllers, services, repositories, and database access.
Provides BaseController patterns for consistent success responses, error handling, and Sentry capture.
Shows Zod validation schemas for request bodies, parameters, query strings, and form models.
Documents unified configuration patterns for INI files, environment overrides, and secret handling.
Gives testing guidance for unit tests, integration tests, mocks, setup, teardown, and authenticated routes.
Includes refactoring examples for moving business logic out of routes into controllers and services.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not execute code or modify projects by itself.
Examples assume Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Prisma, Zod, Jest, and Sentry conventions.
Some examples use project-specific names that users must adapt to their own services.
Monitoring examples need privacy review before production use with sensitive request data.

use_cases.title: Create a new API feature
use_cases.description: Use the route, controller, service, repository, validation, and test checklists to implement a feature with consistent boundaries.

use_cases.title: Refactor route-heavy services
use_cases.description: Use the anti-patterns and refactoring examples to move business logic out of Express routes safely.

use_cases.title: Standardize service practices
use_cases.description: Use the configuration, monitoring, middleware, and testing guidance to align multiple TypeScript microservices.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a backend feature
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the backend-dev-guidelines skill to outline the route, controller, service, repository, validation, monitoring, and test work for this feature: [feature description].

prompt_templates.title: Review an Express route
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the backend-dev-guidelines skill to review this Express route for misplaced business logic, missing validation, weak error handling, and missing tests: [paste route].

prompt_templates.title: Design service boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the backend-dev-guidelines skill to design controller, service, repository, DTO, and Zod schema boundaries for this workflow: [workflow].

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a legacy module
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the backend-dev-guidelines skill to create a step-by-step refactor plan for this legacy backend module. Preserve behavior and add focused tests: [module summary].

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to add a user creation endpoint.
output: Route delegates to a controller and applies authentication and audit middleware.
Controller validates input, calls the service, returns a created response, and handles errors through BaseController.
Service enforces duplicate email and role rules, then calls the repository.
Tests cover success, duplicate email, invalid roles, and controller error responses.

output_examples.input: A team asks how to migrate direct process.env usage.
output: List current environment variable reads and group them by database, server, token, and integration settings.
Create a typed unified configuration object with startup validation and development defaults.
Replace scattered reads with configuration properties and add tests for missing required secrets.

output_examples.input: A reviewer asks whether a route file follows the guidelines.
output: The route should only declare paths, middleware, and controller delegation.
Move validation and business rules into the controller and service layers.
Add focused unit tests for service rules and integration tests for the HTTP contract.

best_practices: Use the architecture overview before adding new files, then open only the focused resource for the task.
Ask the assistant to preserve existing behavior when refactoring routes into controllers and services.
Review monitoring and configuration examples for privacy, secret handling, and production defaults.
anti_patterns: Do not copy examples without adapting project-specific imports, paths, service names, and configuration sources.
Do not use the skill for frontend, data science, or non-TypeScript backend architectures without extra guidance.
Do not enable broad monitoring data capture in production without a privacy and retention review.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands or change files?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill that guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code while they work in a project.

faq.question: Which backend stack does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Prisma, Zod, Jest, and Sentry based microservices.

faq.question: Can it help refactor legacy route files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for moving route logic into controllers, services, repositories, and validation schemas.

faq.question: Does it include database guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents Prisma repositories, transactions, query optimization, and testing with database access.

faq.question: Is it safe for production security decisions?
faq.answer: It can support reviews, but teams should still perform normal security, privacy, and threat model checks.

faq.question: Why were many static findings reported?
faq.answer: The scanner matched Markdown examples, relative imports, environment variable examples, and local URLs as risky patterns.
